content:
# Genesis Chapter 23 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 12v–13r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_023_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_023_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0022 (page 18) and https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0023 (page 19)

---

## Sarah's Death and Burial at Machpelah

**1** Sarah lived to be one hundred and twenty-seven years old. **2** She died at Kiriath-arba — that is, Hebron — in the land of Canaan, and Abraham came to mourn for her and to weep over her.

---

**3** When Abraham had finished his grieving, he rose and spoke to the Hittites, the people of the land. He said, **4** "I am a stranger among you, a foreigner with no land of my own. Let me buy a burial plot from you, so that I can bury my dead and lay her to rest."

**5** The Hittites answered Abraham, **6** "Sir, you are a great prince among us — a man favored by God. Take the finest of our burial sites. Not one of us would refuse you his tomb for burying your dead."

**7** Abraham rose and bowed to the Hittites, the people of the land. **8** He said to them, "If you truly want to help me bury my dead, then please hear me out. Speak on my behalf to Ephron son of Zohar. **9** Ask him to sell me the cave of Machpelah, which is at the edge of his field. Have him sell it to me here, in front of all of you, at the full price — so that I may have a proper burial site."

---

**10** Now Ephron was sitting there among his own people. He spoke up in front of all the Hittites gathered at the city gate: **11** "No, sir — hear me, my lord. I give you the field. The cave that's in it — I give you that too. I am giving it to you in front of all my people. Bury your dead."

**12** Abraham bowed before the people of the land **13** and said to Ephron, there in their hearing, "Please, let me pay you the full price of the field. Take my money, and I will bury my dead there."

**14** Ephron answered Abraham, **15** "My lord, listen to me — what is four hundred shekels of silver between you and me? It is nothing. Go ahead and bury your dead."

**16** Abraham agreed to Ephron's price and weighed out the silver — four hundred shekels of silver at the going merchant rate. He paid it in full in front of the Hittites.

---

**17** And so the field of Ephron at Machpelah, east of Mamre — the field itself, the cave within it, and every tree in the field, within all its boundaries all around — **18** passed legally to Abraham as his own property, witnessed by all the Hittites who had gathered at the city gate.

**19** After that, Abraham buried his wife Sarah in the cave of Machpelah east of Mamre — that is, Hebron — in the land of Canaan. **20** The field and the cave within it were transferred from the Hittites to Abraham as a permanent burial site.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 23 — the other side of grief

Chapter 22 ended suspended at its height: the covenant reaffirmed with an oath, Abraham returning from Moriah, Beer-sheba as home. Chapter 23 begins in the valley. Sarah is dead. We are not told how she died. We are not told how old Abraham is or where he was when she died (the text says he *came* to mourn, suggesting he had to travel). We are not even told, in the narrative, whether Abraham mourned because he loved her — the text simply says he came and wept. These things are given without color commentary, in the characteristic Hebrew way. The reader supplies the rest.

What follows her death is not elegiac — it is transactional. The entire chapter is a negotiation. And it is one of the most brilliantly rendered negotiations in ancient literature.

---

### Verse 1 — "the years of the life of Sarah"

The Hebrew for verse 1 is unusually structured: **מֵאָה שָׁנָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי שָׂרָה** — "a hundred years and twenty years and seven years — the years of the life of Sarah." The years are listed in descending order (hundreds, twenties, sevens) and the phrase *shney chayyei Sarah* ("the years of the life of Sarah") closes the sentence like a seal. The repetition is not careless; it is liturgical. It honors her. Sarah is the only woman in the Hebrew scriptures whose lifespan is explicitly recorded. That itself tells us something about her standing in the narrative.

The rabbis interpreted the unusual counting (not "127 years" but the three-unit form) as suggesting that at 100 she was as sinless as at 20, and at 20 she was as beautiful as at 7. Whether or not that reading is intended, the structure signals that this is not a routine genealogical notation. It is a memorial.

---

### Verse 2 — the small kaf and the public man weeping

**וַיָּבֹא אַבְרָהָם לִסְפֹּד לְשָׂרָה וְלִבְכֹּתָהּ** — "Abraham came to mourn for Sarah and to weep for her."

The Masoretic tradition marks an unusual scribal feature here: the כ (*kaf*) in **וְלִבְכֹּתָהּ** (*velivkotah*, "and to weep for her") is written small — a *kaf ketannah*. The rabbis discussed it: perhaps because weeping is only secondary to mourning, or perhaps signaling something constrained in the grief. I do not render this feature visibly in translation, but it is worth noting. In the manuscript visible before me in the Leningrad Codex (folio 12v), the diminished kaf is present and perceptible.

The phrase "came to mourn" uses the verb **סָפַד** (*safad*) — formal lamentation, the public ritual of mourning — followed by **בָּכָה** (*bakha*), to weep, which is the private, uncontrolled expression. Abraham does both: he performs the rites *and* he weeps. The great patriarch, covenant-bearer, man tested on Moriah — he weeps for his wife. The text does not hedge this or explain it. It simply says it.

---

### Verse 4 — *ger ve-toshav*: the foreigner's position

**גֵּר-וְתוֹשָׁב אָנֹכִי עִמָּכֶם** — "a sojourner and a resident I am among you."

This phrase is the key that unlocks the whole transaction. Abraham identifies himself as *ger-ve-toshav* — a compound status term that is legally significant. A *ger* (sojourner, alien) has no inherent land rights; a *toshav* (resident, settler) has some degree of established presence. The combination acknowledges his liminal position: he has lived among the Hittites long enough to be known and respected, but he does not own land. He has no burial ground. Without land, he cannot give Sarah a proper, permanent resting place.

The entire negotiation flows from this opening admission of vulnerability. Abraham is not demanding. He is asking. And the way he frames the ask — *let me buy* a burial plot — immediately signals that he intends to pay, not receive a gift.

This legal realism is characteristic of Genesis's treatment of the patriarchs in Canaan. They are there by divine promise but not yet by legal possession. The purchase of Machpelah is, in some sense, the first down payment on the land.

---

### Verse 6 — *nesi' Elohim*: "great prince" or "prince of God"?

**נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה בְּתוֹכֵנוּ** — literally "a prince of God you are among us."

This is the Hittites' assessment of Abraham. The phrase *nesi' Elohim* is a Hebrew superlative construction: *Elohim* (God) is used as a kind of intensifier — "a godly prince," "a mighty prince," "a prince in the highest sense." I have translated it "a great prince among us — a man favored by God" to capture both the social standing (he is a recognized great man) and the religious undertone (they seem to be acknowledging something extraordinary about him, perhaps his reputation from the covenant events).

The Hittites are being gracious here, but gracious in the calculated way of ancient Near Eastern diplomacy. They honor Abraham publicly, call him great, tell him he can use *any* of their tombs. What they do not do is name a price or offer to sell. The offer to give freely is itself a negotiating move: it creates social obligation without transferring ownership. Abraham understands this perfectly.

---

### Verses 7–9 — the structure of ANE bargaining

What follows is a masterpiece of ancient Near Eastern negotiation rhetoric, and it must be read as such to make full sense.

**Round 1:** The Hittites offer everything — use any of our tombs, take what you want, we won't refuse you. (Verse 6). This is magnanimous theater. In the actual social framework, accepting this "gift" would put Abraham in their debt and give him no permanent ownership. He refuses the gift by redirecting the conversation.

**Round 2:** Abraham bows (verse 7 — respect maintained, dominance declined) and then asks not the whole group but specifically for Ephron's cave, at the full price. (Verses 8–9). He names the price voluntarily: *besef male* — "at the full silver/price." By saying "I will pay full price" before any number is mentioned, he preemptively forecloses the possibility of a gift or token price.

**Round 3:** Ephron (verses 10–11) gives Abraham everything *publicly* — the field, the cave, everything, as a gift, in front of witnesses. This is the most face-saving and socially binding move possible: now Abraham must either accept the "gift" (and be in Ephron's debt before the watching community) or refuse a public gesture of generosity (which carries social cost). Abraham refuses graciously.

**Round 4:** Abraham, bowing again, insists on paying in front of everyone. (Verses 12–13). The phrase "take my money" is direct and irrefusable in context.

**Round 5:** Ephron, now out of gracious maneuvers, names the price — but names it with elaborate self-minimization: "What is 400 shekels between you and me? It is nothing." (Verse 15). The number is actually high — 400 shekels of silver is a substantial sum, likely well above market value (cf. Jeremiah 32:9, where Jeremiah buys a field for 17 shekels). Ephron minimizes it socially even as he names it, which allows him to maintain the fiction of generosity while extracting a significant price.

**Round 6:** Abraham pays immediately, without haggling, at the named price. (Verse 16). He has what he came for.

This is not a simple purchase. It is a performance of honor and counter-honor, generosity and counter-generosity, in which both parties maintain maximum face while a real transaction takes place underneath. The social script and the economic reality run on parallel tracks. The narrator renders it with complete deadpan accuracy, leaving the reader to appreciate the dance.

---

### Verse 9 — the cave of Machpelah

**מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה** — "the cave of Machpelah."

The name *Machpelah* is likely derived from **כָּפַל** (*kafal*), "to double" — suggesting a double cave, or a cave with two chambers. The tradition is that multiple patriarchal couples were buried there (Abraham and Sarah, Isaac and Rebekah, Jacob and Leah — Genesis 49:29-32). Whatever the original topography, the cave became the patriarchal burial site par excellence, and its location at Hebron is identified today with the Cave of the Patriarchs (Ibrahimi Mosque), one of the most contested holy sites in the world. The purchase of this ground in Genesis 23 is its founding title deed.

---

### Verse 16 — "current money with the merchant"

**עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר** — literally "passing to/for the merchant" — a technical commercial phrase indicating silver weighed according to the accepted standard of the trading community. I have rendered it "at the going merchant rate" to convey the legal precision of the phrase without the awkwardness of a literal rendering. This is a real commercial transaction with real commercial language. The narrator's inclusion of this detail — the weight standard, the specific amount — signals that this is legally binding in the ancient Near Eastern sense: witnessed, properly compensated, documented by social memory.

---

### Verses 17–18 — the property description

The legal description in verses 17–18 is deliberately formulaic. It names:
1. The field of Ephron at Machpelah
2. East of Mamre (geographical anchor)
3. The field and the cave in it
4. All the trees in the field, within all its boundaries

This is ancient property-transfer language. Every element matters. Trees were separately valued in ancient Near Eastern law. "Within all its boundaries all around" is a standard conveyance formula. The narrator is rendering a legal document into narrative, and the legal specificity is the point: Abraham now owns this land. Completely. Permanently. Witnessed.

---

### Verse 19 — Sarah is buried

**וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן קָבַר אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ** — "And after this Abraham buried Sarah his wife."

After twenty verses of legal negotiation, the actual burial is a single clause. The whole machinery of the chapter — the mourning, the approach to the Hittites, the elaborate negotiation, the payment, the property transfer — has been for this moment. And the moment itself is given almost no description: *he buried her.* That is enough.

The location is restated fully: the cave of Machpelah, east of Mamre, that is Hebron, in the land of Canaan. It is given as formally as the deed. Because it *is* the deed. The burial is the act that consummates the purchase. The cave is now Sarah's resting place, Abraham's property, the patriarchal grave.

---

### Verse 2 and verse 19 — the chapter's frame

The chapter opens with Abraham coming to mourn and weep (**v. 2**) and closes with Abraham burying Sarah in the land he has now legally secured (**v. 19**). Between those two moments is the entire negotiation. The grief is the engine of the transaction; the transaction is the completion of the grief. He cannot simply weep: he must act. He must do the thing that honoring the dead requires — give her a place, a proper place, a permanent place, a place that will still be hers in a hundred years.

This is how Genesis handles love: not in declarations but in action. Abraham does not speak of his love for Sarah anywhere in chapter 23. He walks into a room full of strangers, identifies himself as a vulnerable foreigner, bows twice, pays a premium price, and gives her a cave in the land of promise.

That is the love story.

---

### The cave as promise

There is a theological dimension to Machpelah that the text allows without pressing. The land of Canaan was promised to Abraham's descendants (Genesis 12:7, 15:18). Abraham himself lived in it as a sojourner, never owning it, dwelling in tents. The purchase of Machpelah is the one piece of Canaan Abraham actually, legally acquires in his lifetime. It is a burial plot. The only land he owns is where he will bury the dead and eventually be buried himself.

The promise of the land is held, in Genesis, by the graves of the patriarchs. Not by conquests or constructions, but by burial. The dead hold the land until the living can return to it. This is a quiet, patient theology — the theology of the long promise, the faith that sees forward across centuries.

Jacob, dying in Egypt, will spend his last breath making his sons swear to carry his bones back to this cave (Genesis 49:29-32). Joseph will make the same request at his death (Genesis 50:24-25). The cave of Machpelah is where the rope of the promise is tied. And it begins with Abraham weeping over his wife, rising from his grief, and going to buy a cave.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 12v–13r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 23 begins in the left column of folio 12v (page 001 for this chapter), immediately following the end of Genesis 22, and continues through folio 13r (page 002), concluding at verse 20 before Genesis 24 begins. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 18–19 (pa_0022–pa_0023; accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0022 and /0023). The Vaticanus LXX reads closely with the Hebrew throughout Genesis 23, with no significant text-critical divergences noted. The LXX renders *ger-ve-toshav* as **πάροικος καὶ παρεπίδημος** ("resident alien and sojourner") and *nesi' Elohim* as **βασιλεὺς παρὰ θεοῦ** ("a king from God"), opting for "king" where the Hebrew has "prince/chief" (*nasi'*). The LXX's choice of *βασιλεύς* (king) rather than *ἄρχων* (ruler/prince) slightly elevates the honorific; this may reflect the Hellenistic translator's social register choices rather than a different Hebrew Vorlage. No DSS fragments of Genesis 23 are extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
